frontmatter.title: Classical Mechanics: From Newton to Hamilton

children.value: Newton’s mechanics describes motion with forces.

children.html: F=maF = maF=ma

children.value: predicts trajectories once you know the forces and the starting conditions.
The Hamiltonian reformulation describes motion with energy.
The same physics is repackaged around the total energy

children.html: H=T+VH = T + VH=T+V

children.value: and the pair of variables position

children.html: qqq
ppp

children.value: Phase space is the natural arena.
A classical state is a single point

children.html: (q,p)(q, p)(q,p)

children.value: , and its motion traces a curve of constant energy.
The Hamiltonian is the doorway to quantum mechanics.
Replacing position and momentum by operators turns

children.html: HHH
H^\hat HH^

children.value: and Hamilton’s equations into the Schrodinger equation.
Before quantum mechanics we need one idea from classical physics done properly: the

. Newton’s laws are what you learned first, but quantum mechanics is not built on forces. It is built on energy, on the variables position and momentum, and on a bracket that becomes the commutator. This page explains why we reformulate classical mechanics and how that reformulation hands us the Schrodinger equation.
Newton’s second law says a force changes a particle’s momentum:

children.html: F=ma=md2xdt2F = ma = m\frac{d^2 x}{dt^2}F=ma=mdt2d2x​

children.value: Give the force and the initial position and velocity, and the trajectory

children.html: x(t)x(t)x(t)

children.value: is fixed for all time. For a mass on a spring the force is

children.html: F=−kxF = -kxF=−kx
x(t)=Acos⁡(ωt)x(t) = A\cos(\omega t)x(t)=Acos(ωt)
ω=k/m\omega = \sqrt{k/m}ω=k/m​

children.value: Two quantities organize the motion. The

children.html: T=12mv2T = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2T=21​mv2
V(x)V(x)V(x)

children.value: , whose negative slope is the force,

children.html: F=−dV/dxF = -dV/dxF=−dV/dx

children.value: , stays constant as the particle moves:

children.html: E=T+V=12mv2+V(x)=constant.E = T + V = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2 + V(x) = \text{constant}.E=T+V=21​mv2+V(x)=constant.

children.value: Newton’s picture is intuitive, but it is awkward for quantum mechanics: it centers on force, a vector that is hard to generalize to abstract coordinates, and it hides the energy, which is the quantity quantum mechanics actually quantizes. We want a formulation built directly on energy.
The trick is to stop using position and velocity, and instead use position

children.html: qqq
p=mvp = mvp=mv

children.value: as the two independent variables. The
is the total energy written in terms of these:

children.html: H(q,p)=T+V=p22m+V(q)H(q, p) = T + V = \frac{p^2}{2m} + V(q)H(q,p)=T+V=2mp2​+V(q)

children.value: All of the dynamics now follows from a single, strikingly symmetric pair of first-order equations:

children.html: dqdt=∂H∂p,dpdt=−∂H∂q\frac{dq}{dt} = \frac{\partial H}{\partial p}, \qquad \frac{dp}{dt} = -\frac{\partial H}{\partial q}dtdq​=∂p∂H​,dtdp​=−∂q∂H​
p=mq˙p = m\dot qp=mq˙​

children.value: ; the second reproduces Newton’s law, since

children.html: −∂H/∂q=−dV/dq=F-\partial H/\partial q = -dV/dq = F−∂H/∂q=−dV/dq=F

children.value: . So Hamilton’s equations contain exactly the same physics as

children.value: Between Newton and Hamilton sits the

children.html: L=T−VL = T - VL=T−V
ddt∂L∂q˙=∂L∂q\frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot q} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial q}dtd​∂q˙​∂L​=∂q∂L​

children.value: : the actual path makes the action

children.html: S=∫L dtS = \int L\,dtS=∫Ldt

children.value: stationary. The Hamiltonian is obtained from the Lagrangian by switching from velocity

children.html: q˙\dot qq˙​
p=∂L/∂q˙p = \partial L/\partial \dot qp=∂L/∂q˙​

children.value: . We go straight to the Hamiltonian because it, not the Lagrangian, is what quantum mechanics quantizes. The least-action idea does return in Feynman’s path-integral formulation.
In the Hamiltonian picture a complete classical state is a single point

children.html: (q,p)(q, p)(q,p)

children.value: , the plane of position versus momentum. As time runs, the point moves along a curve, and because energy is conserved that curve is a contour of constant

children.html: HHH
H=p22m+12mω2q2H = \frac{p^2}{2m} + \frac{1}{2}m\omega^2 q^2H=2mp2​+21​mω2q2

children.value: . A low-energy state traces a small ellipse near the origin; higher energy means a bigger ellipse.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

m, omega = 1.0, 1.0
q = np.linspace(-3, 3, 400)
p = np.linspace(-3, 3, 400)
Q, P = np.meshgrid(q, p)
H = P**2 / (2 * m) + 0.5 * m * omega**2 * Q**2

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4.2))

# Phase-space energy contours (ellipses)
levels = [0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 1.8, 2.8]
cs = axes[0].contour(Q, P, H, levels=levels, cmap='viridis')
axes[0].clabel(cs, fmt='E=%.1f', fontsize=8)
# One trajectory, arrow shows clockwise flow
E = 1.0
theta = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 200)
axes[0].plot(np.sqrt(2 * E) * np.cos(theta), np.sqrt(2 * E) * np.sin(theta), 'k', lw=1)
axes[0].annotate('', xy=(np.sqrt(2 * E) * np.cos(0.3), np.sqrt(2 * E) * np.sin(0.3)),
                 xytext=(np.sqrt(2 * E), 0.0),
                 arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='k'))
axes[0].set_xlabel('position q')
axes[0].set_ylabel('momentum p')
axes[0].set_aspect('equal')
axes[0].set_title('Fig.1a Phase space: energy contours are ellipses')

# Time view: position and momentum oscillate, 90 deg out of phase
t = np.linspace(0, 4 * np.pi, 400)
axes[1].plot(t, np.sqrt(2 * E) * np.cos(t), color='#2e4057', lw=2, label='q(t)')
axes[1].plot(t, -np.sqrt(2 * E) * np.sin(t), color='#d1495b', lw=2, label='p(t)')
axes[1].axhline(0, color='gray', lw=0.6)
axes[1].set_xlabel('time t')
axes[1].legend(fontsize=9)
axes[1].set_title('Fig.1b Newton view: q and p oscillate in time')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The two panels are the same motion in two languages: Hamilton’s phase-space ellipse (left) and Newton’s time trajectories (right). Quantum mechanics will replace the sharp phase-space point with a spread-out probability cloud, because position and momentum can no longer be pinned down at once.
Why the Hamiltonian is the bridge to quantum mechanics

children.label: Why the Hamiltonian is the bridge to quantum mechanics

children.value: Three features of the Hamiltonian formulation carry over almost verbatim into quantum mechanics.

children.html: HHH

children.value: is the energy. The quantum Hamiltonian

children.html: H^\hat HH^

children.value: is what appears in the Schrodinger equation, and its eigenvalues are the allowed energy levels.
A classical observable is any function of

children.value: gives the Schrodinger equation. The classical Hamiltonian literally becomes the quantum Hamiltonian by substituting operators for

children.html: qqq
ppp

children.value: The Poisson bracket becomes the commutator.
Classical mechanics has a bracket that measures how two observables co-vary,

\{A, B\} = \frac{\partial A}{\partial q}\frac{\partial B}{\partial p} - \frac{\partial A}{\partial p}\frac{\partial B}{\partial q},
\qquad \text{so}\quad \{q, p\} = 1.
children.html: {A,B}=∂A∂q∂B∂p−∂A∂p∂B∂q,so{q,p}=1.\{A, B\} = \frac{\partial A}{\partial q}\frac{\partial B}{\partial p} - \frac{\partial A}{\partial p}\frac{\partial B}{\partial q}, \qquad \text{so}\quad \{q, p\} = 1.{A,B}=∂q∂A​∂p∂B​−∂p∂A​∂q∂B​,so{q,p}=1.

children.value: Quantization promotes this bracket to the

children.html: {A,B}→1iℏ[A^,B^]\{A,B\} \to \frac{1}{i\hbar}[\hat A, \hat B]{A,B}→iℏ1​[A^,B^]
{q,p}=1\{q,p\}=1{q,p}=1

children.value: [\hat x, \hat p] = \hat x\hat p - \hat p\hat x = i\hbar
children.html: [x^,p^]=x^p^−p^x^=iℏ[\hat x, \hat p] = \hat x\hat p - \hat p\hat x = i\hbar[x^,p^​]=x^p^​−p^​x^=iℏ

children.value: This single non-zero commutator is the seed of the uncertainty principle: because

children.html: x^\hat xx^
p^\hat pp^​

children.value: do not commute, no state can have a definite position and a definite momentum at the same time. The
page showed why non-commuting operators behave this way; here we see where that particular commutator comes from.
